text:
Urban Exploration (URBEX), the practice of investigating abandoned man-made structures and brownfields, has evolved from a niche subculture into a global phenomenon and a specialized form of non-consumption tourism. Despite its growing popularity, the legal relationship between the property owner and the explorer remains complex and under-researched. This paper examines the legal aspects of URBEX through the lens of the property owner within the framework of the Czech legal system. Utilizing a theoretical analysis of legislative sources, the study evaluates owner rights and obligations across three dimensions: civil, administrative, and criminal law. 

In the civil sphere, the research focuses on the "ownership entails obligations" principle, exploring the owner's right to protection against unauthorized entry via negatory actions, as well as their liability for damages resulting from building defects or neglect. From an administrative perspective, the paper details the statutory duties imposed by the Building Act and health and safety regulations, particularly regarding the maintenance and securing of brownfields and construction sites. Finally, the paper investigates potential criminal liability, hypothesizing how negligence-based offenses against life and health might apply to owners who fail to secure hazardous properties. Given the current lack of established judicial precedent in this specific area, this study provides a foundational theoretical framework for understanding the normative boundaries of property ownership in the age of modern urban exploration.
